title: The Shocking Information Revealed by Acharya!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation omitted by OCR has been restored, garbled English quotation text has been transcribed from the printed page, and clear OCR character, word-break, and spacing errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: P. V. Acharya, who served as Special Public Prosecutor in the disproportionate-assets case against Jayalalithaa and the other accused before the Bengaluru Special Court, has written an English account of his life titled All from Memory. Karnataka High Court Chief Justice Vaghela released it in Bengaluru on 15 November 2014. Justice Venugopal and former Union Law Minister Veerappa Moily attended the event.

The book contains thirty-three chapters and 336 pages. Its twenty-fifth chapter is titled “Case Against Selvi J. Jayalalitha, Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu.” On pages 206 to 220, Acharya, a senior advocate who served five times as Karnataka's Advocate General, recounts his experiences in the case. I want to present only some important points from that account.

The trial began in Chennai. The prosecution examined 250 witnesses and entered more than two thousand documents. Before its evidence concluded, Jayalalithaa became Chief Minister. Her Government appointed a new prosecutor. Seventy-six witnesses who had already testified were recalled, and they repudiated their earlier testimony. The newly appointed prosecutor neither cross-examined them nor treated them as hostile. As the Supreme Court observed, the Chennai proceedings became a “farce of a trial.”
After the case was transferred to Bengaluru, Acharya applied to examine another forty-five witnesses. Only twenty-one were recalled, and even that took a long time.

Under normal trial procedure, the accused then had to be examined under Section 313 of the Code of Criminal Procedure. The Supreme Court had clearly directed every accused to appear personally. Yet securing their presence became extremely difficult.

After Jayalalithaa became Chief Minister on 16 May 2011, Tamil Nadu's Directorate of Vigilance and Anti-Corruption completely changed its approach towards Acharya and the prosecution. It proposed further investigation under Section 173(8) and sought to appoint a separate lawyer for High Court proceedings. Acharya opposed both positions on legal grounds.

Because of the Supreme Court's order, the DVAC could not bypass the Special Public Prosecutor by appointing its own counsel. Correspondence between Acharya and the DVAC was filed in the High Court. After hearing the matter, the High Court upheld Acharya's stand and clarified that the Special Public Prosecutor alone could represent the DVAC.

The DVAC challenged that order in the Supreme Court. Acharya appeared there. On 30 January 2012, after hearing both sides in detail, the Supreme Court dismissed the special-leave petitions and affirmed the Karnataka High Court's order.

Although the Supreme Court required all accused to appear personally to record statements under Section 313, Jayalalithaa sought exemption. She asked to submit a written statement or be examined by video. Her application relied on an earlier permission granted by the Chennai Special Court to answer questions without appearing. The Supreme Court had strongly objected to that permission.

After both the trial court and High Court rejected her request, Jayalalithaa approached the Supreme Court. It too rejected her plea and ordered her to appear and answer questions. Only after all this did she appear before the Bengaluru Special Court on 23 November 2011.

Acharya writes that his fair and honest positions, and the courts' refusal to permit further investigation, separate DVAC counsel, or exemption from appearance, caused serious setbacks to the accused. Their followers and supporters, whom he calls “vested interests,” appeared deeply annoyed and disturbed.
Those vested interests concluded that Acharya, as Special Public Prosecutor, was the principal cause of their setbacks and persistently tried to remove him.
Because he simultaneously served as Special Public Prosecutor and Advocate General, attempts were made to force him out by arguing that one person should not hold both posts.

Two writ petitions alleged irregularities in the BMS Educational Trust, which he chaired, and sought a CBI inquiry in the hope that it would remove him from the prosecution. The High Court dismissed both petitions and later dismissed the writ appeals as well.

After failing to obtain what they wanted from the High Court or Governor, political pressure followed. Since Acharya held two posts, efforts were made to have the BJP high command direct the State Government to ask him to step down as Special Public Prosecutor.
Under this continuing pressure, Acharya decided instead to resign as Advocate General. He resigned on 8 February 2012, and the State Government accepted it the same day.

After every effort to throw him out as prosecutor failed, the vested interests apparently decided to use a private complaint. A member of that group told him that unless he immediately resigned, a complaint would be filed and vigorously pursued.

The court order for investigation caused Acharya mental distress. After more than fifty-six years of unblemished legal service, he feared damage to his reputation. He believed this was their final weapon. He decided that he would leave the prosecutor's post, but not before proving his innocence.

After a full hearing, the Karnataka High Court dismissed the private complaint and quashed the investigation. It held that the complaint disclosed no offence justifying an investigation and that the Special Judge lacked jurisdiction to entertain it.
Acharya writes: “During the pendency of the above criminal proceedings against me, naturally I had undergone mental agony.”

Had he resigned earlier, he says, the vested interests would have achieved through pressure what their many tactics could not. He therefore refused to resign until the High Court cleared him. After it quashed the complaint, he submitted his resignation on 13 August 2012.

The Chief Justice of the Karnataka High Court strongly wished him to reconsider. But in his state of mind Acharya was determined not to withdraw it. The State Government finally accepted his resignation on 17 January 2013. His connection with the case ended. He says this was the only occasion in his legal career when he could not complete a responsibility entrusted to him.

He lists the many obstructions encountered in the case. Appearing in the trial taught him an extraordinary range of novel grounds on which adjournments could be secured. He says he could write a book titled The Law of Adjournments, but does not wish to, lest an accused person use it to prolong another trial.

Udanpirappē, according to Acharya's account, the only “reward” he received for serving honestly and conscientiously, and refusing to bend to unlawful demands from Jayalalithaa's side, was mental agony. Persistent harassment and political pressure ultimately forced his resignation. This was one among the strange events in the disproportionate-assets case.

The courts faced adjournment after adjournment until a Bengaluru Special Judge asked, “Is this a criminal court or an adjournment court?” Again and again, on one pretext or another, legal loopholes were used to take matters to the High Court and Supreme Court, drawing repeated judicial criticism.

Even at the final stage, they sought changes to the judgment date and court premises. After those were accepted, they approached the Supreme Court seeking judgment in another State, and that request too was dismissed.

The situation reached the point where Special Judge Pachhapure warned that he would deliver judgment based on the accused's failure to appear. He expressed his anguish: “I have been sitting alone in court for the past six months. I feel as though I have been placed in solitary confinement.”

Those who subjected Special Court judges and people such as P. V. Acharya to such anguish now dress themselves as paragons of virtue and attempt to deceive the world. What reason can there be except the belief that Tamil people are gullible enough to think everything white is milk?
With affection,
M.K.
